Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I work part-time and have limited resources, so I had to fit studying into whatever gaps I could find. I'm a hands-on learner, so I tried to do practical exercises whenever possible rather than just reading. I'd often study late at night after work, sometimes at the library when I could get there. With 17 online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about which ones got my attention each week. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and my work schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then I'd have intense catch-up sessions when something was due. I tried to block out Sunday afternoons for studying, but that didn't always happen. My anxiety about falling behind would spike, and then I'd cram for hours to compensate. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but with everything going on, regularity felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q3: Usually a looming deadline or a notification about something due would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my attendance was so spotty. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me to check in more often because earning those badges gave me small wins. When I was feeling particularly anxious about my grades, I'd compulsively check my progress even if I didn't do much once I was there.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester when the gamified elements made logging in feel almost fun, like a game I wanted to check on. I'd wake up and quickly look at my progress before work. But honestly, it never became truly routine because my life was too chaotic. The closest I got was checking in every few days during that gamification period, but once the novelty wore off and my anxiety spiked again, it became more sporadic.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful because I could watch them on my phone during breaks at work or on the bus. I watched over 190 minutes of video content because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. The discussion posts helped too because interacting with peers made concepts stick better for me. Anything hands-on or interactive worked well with how I learn, much better than just reading dense text.
Q6: I often ignored the lengthy reading materials and supplementary PDFs because I just didn't have time to sit and read everything. Some of the discussion prompts felt too abstract or theoretical, so I'd skip those. I delayed a lot of the smaller assignments because they didn't seem as urgent as the bigger ones, which is probably why my completion rate was so low. When you're stretched thin on resources and time, you have to make tough choices about what to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate practical application or if it contributed directly to my grade. The gamified tasks felt worthwhile because I got instant feedback and could see my progress visually. Peer interactions were valuable because I learned from others' perspectives and felt less alone in struggling. If something felt like busywork or didn't connect to real understanding, I'd mentally check out even if I technically completed it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on, honestly. With required work piling up and my limited time, optional stuff always got pushed aside. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them because they looked interesting, but survival mode kicked in. Required activities came with the pressure of grades, which both motivated me and increased my anxiety. The gamified optional tasks were the exception because they felt rewarding without the stress of being graded.
Q9: Absolutely, I did so much studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't afford to buy them all, and I'd study from those for hours. I also made handwritten flashcards and practiced with them during my commute. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from printed notes or downloaded materials while my LMS showed zero activity. I'd meet with study groups in person and we'd work through problems together for hours. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it mentally while doing dishes or walking to work. My kinesthetic learning style meant I often learned by doing physical practice problems on paper, none of which would register anywhere digitally.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work from those files offline for days. My internet access isn't always stable, so I learned to grab what I needed in one session. I'd annotate PDFs on my phone and review them repeatedly without ever logging back in. The LMS probably thought I glanced at something once when really I studied it for hours offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't face the coursework. When I saw how behind I was on assignments, it felt paralyzing rather than motivating. Financial stress would hit and I'd have to pick up extra shifts at work, leaving no energy for studying. The sadness would creep in and I'd feel like what was even the point. Those were the weeks my attendance dropped to almost nothing.
Q13: All of those factors affected me constantly. Fatigue from working and studying was my baseline state, honestly. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because logging in just reminded me of what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities meant I was always triaging between work, courses, and basic self-care. The cognitive load of managing 17 courses with low resources was genuinely overwhelming at times.
Q14: There were definitely times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or just zoning out from exhaustion. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd log in and click around to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really learning. My body was present but my mind had checked out, especially during high-anxiety periods.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes, like if I didn't start, I couldn't fail. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same. The bigger or more complex an assignment seemed, the more I'd avoid it. It's a terrible cycle because the postponing just made my anxiety worse, but knowing that didn't stop me from doing it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming and panicking. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and focus on other things. The problem was that by the time I'd finish one deadline, another had already passed or was imminent. Deadlines created structure but also enormous stress, especially when multiple courses had them at the same time.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it genuinely lifted my mood and made me want to engage more. But feedback often came too late to help me improve on the next thing. Critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety spike and I'd avoid that course for a while. The gamified task feedback was immediate and visual, which worked better for me than written comments I'd get days later. I needed that instant reinforcement to stay motivated.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the sheer volume across all my courses was paralyzing. When structure was rigid with strict deadlines, I struggled because my life doesn't allow for that consistency. More flexible structures let me work in my own chaotic rhythm. The gamified elements added structure that felt supportive rather than punishing, which made a real difference.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks with rewards. Long, dense modules without clear checkpoints made engagement harder because I'd lose track of where I was. Interactive elements worked well for my learning style, while walls of text made me disengage. Having peer interaction options helped because I felt less isolated. The lack of offline-friendly design made things harder given my internet situation.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, actually, browsing and watching and participating in discussions but struggling to complete actual assignments. High anxiety can make you engage with materials obsessively while being too paralyzed to produce work. Maybe they're learning a lot but can't translate that into submitted assignments. Or they're using the LMS as a way to feel productive without facing the harder task of actually completing things. It's a coping mechanism, honestly.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was low and my attendance was poor. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in any log. Some people are efficient learners who don't need to spend hours in the LMS to understand material. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials, textbooks, or peer discussions outside the platform. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you engage with different materials. They might catch declining engagement or deadline-driven behavior. But they miss all the offline learning, the emotional state behind the clicks, and the quality of attention. They can't tell if someone watched a video while fully focused or while half-asleep. They miss the anxiety, the sadness, the financial stress that shapes everything. Numbers without context are misleading.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to get grades. It's when I'm curious and asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, it's hands-on involvement, discussing with peers, applying what I learn. It's emotional investment too, feeling like the course matters to my future. True engagement isn't about time spent logged in; it's about mental and emotional presence. Some of my most engaged moments happened nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought my anxiety to even open my laptop some days. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with borrowed textbooks or handwritten notes. It can't see that I work part-time and have limited resources, which shapes everything about how I study. My resilience in pushing through despite feeling sad and overwhelmed doesn't register anywhere. The gamified tasks genuinely improved my mood and motivation, but that internal shift is invisible in the data. My engagement was real, just not always visible.
